content.text: Tracking behavioural changes in an AI service demands more than conventional version history. When a prompt update shifts response tone, or an evaluation pass rate drops two points, those changes must be surfaced in plain language so every stakeholder — engineers, product managers, and API consumers — can act on the information. Automated changelog generation turns the commit graph into that plain-language record without requiring a human to write release notes by hand.

attrs.alt: Automated changelog and release notes pipeline: Git commits flow through the conventional commit parser and prompt registry, into the changelog generator producing CHANGELOG.md sections, which are rendered as audience-specific release notes and published as a GitHub Release with a quality gate.

content.text: Why Changelogs Matter More for AI Services
In a traditional web service, a changelog entry for

is self-explanatory — a bug in authentication logic was corrected. An AI service is different because the service's behaviour is not fully described by its code. The system prompts, the model version, the temperature, and the evaluation suite all govern what the service actually does. A deployment that changes only a prompt file will produce zero code diff but may alter every response the service ever returns.

This asymmetry between source-code change and behavioural change makes a human-readable changelog essential for AI services. Downstream integrations — other services that call the AI API, product flows that parse structured outputs, safety monitors that flag unexpected categories — all depend on the service behaving consistently. When behaviour changes, those integrations need a concrete record they can act on: what changed, when, and by how much. Without an automated changelog that captures prompt changes and evaluation metrics alongside code changes, that record exists only in someone's memory or a buried Slack thread.

The operational cost of a missing changelog becomes visible at incident time. When a customer reports that responses suddenly include a disclaimer they never asked for, the first question is "what changed between version X and version Y?" If the only record is a git log of code changes, and the prompt update was not tracked, the investigation has no starting point. An automated changelog that includes every prompt diff and every evaluation run eliminates that blind spot.
What Goes in an AI Service Changelog
The changelog for an AI service needs entries that are meaningless in a conventional software project. The full list of categories, with the rationale for each:
— new capabilities, new tools registered to the agent, new output modes. Described in plain language: what the agent can now do that it could not before.

— every modified prompt file, identified by its SHA-256 content hash before and after. The hash allows an operator to verify which prompt version is running and to reproduce exact behaviour by pinning to a hash.
— added guardrails, updated refusal patterns, tightened scope constraints. These appear first in the changelog so they are never buried under feature entries.
— pass rate deltas across named eval suites, with the baseline and new value. A regression here is as significant as a bug fix.

— when the underlying model is updated (e.g., moving from one minor revision to the next), the changelog records the old and new model IDs. Behaviour can shift substantially across model versions even with identical prompts.
— incorrect tool invocation logic, malformed structured outputs, retry loops that consumed excessive tokens, etc.
— changes to integration guides, prompt authoring notes, or API reference. Low priority but included for completeness.
Conventional Commits as the Source of Truth

Conventional Commits give every commit a machine-readable type and an optional scope. The changelog generator reads that structure to decide which section of the changelog each commit belongs to. The mapping for an AI service extends the standard set:
The commit scope provides additional routing information.
routes to the Prompt Changes section with scope
routes to Bug Fixes. The generator can filter by scope to produce per-component release notes for a monorepo containing multiple AI services.
The Prompt Registry Diff as a Changelog Section

A prompt commit tells you that a prompt changed, but not what changed inside it. For operators who build integrations on top of the AI service, the meaningful information is: which prompt template was altered, what was its content hash before, and what is it now. That hash pair gives them a reproducible reference they can use to roll back or reproduce behaviour.

The changelog generator compares the current prompt registry state against the previous tag. For each changed prompt file it computes SHA-256 of the full content (including any version metadata embedded in the file header). The Prompt Changes section lists each modified prompt by name, its before hash, its after hash, and the one-line description from the commit body. Engineers who need to understand the actual edit can
to see the full text change.
An important discipline: if a prompt changed but the commit message does not use the

type, the automated system will not include it in the Prompt Changes section. Enforcing commit type through a pre-commit hook (see the companion sub-subtopic on conventional commits in GenAI repos) prevents this silent omission.
Eval Metric Trend as a Changelog Section

Evaluation results are not just developer feedback — they are release-quality signals. A changelog that shows a 4.2% improvement in the summarisation accuracy eval, or a 1.1% regression in the safety classification eval, gives product managers and API consumers concrete data to act on.
The eval section in the changelog is produced by comparing the

artifact from the current pipeline run against the same artifact stored for the previous release tag. Each named eval suite produces one line: the suite name, the baseline pass rate, the new pass rate, and the delta. A negative delta is highlighted as a regression. If the regression exceeds a configured threshold, the quality gate (described in the final section) blocks the release.
The following script reads the git log since the last tag, cross-references the prompt registry diff, and writes sections to
. It is designed to run inside a GitHub Actions workflow triggered on tag push or push to main.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""generate-changelog.py — produce CHANGELOG.md entries for an AI service.

Usage:
  python generate-changelog.py --from v1.3.0 --to HEAD --version v1.4.0

Requires: git, python 3.10+, no third-party dependencies.
"""
import subprocess
import hashlib
import json
import sys
import re
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date

COMMIT_PATTERN = re.compile(
    r'^(?P<type>feat|fix|prompt|eval|safety|model|chore|docs|refactor|test)'
    r'(?:\((?P<scope>[^)]+)\))?'
    r'(?P<breaking>!)?:\s+(?P<subject>.+)$'
)

SECTION_ORDER = [
    "Safety", "Breaking Changes", "Features", "Prompt Changes",
    "Bug Fixes", "Eval Metrics", "Model Version", "Docs"
]

TYPE_TO_SECTION = {
    "safety": "Safety",
    "feat": "Features",
    "fix": "Bug Fixes",
    "prompt": "Prompt Changes",
    "eval": "Eval Metrics",
    "model": "Model Version",
    "docs": "Docs",
}


def git_log_since(from_ref: str, to_ref: str) -> list[dict]:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", f"{from_ref}..{to_ref}",
         "--format=%H%x00%s%x00%b%x01"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    commits = []
    for block in result.stdout.split("\x01"):
        block = block.strip()
        if not block:
            continue
        parts = block.split("\x00", 2)
        if len(parts) < 2:
            continue
        sha, subject = parts[0].strip(), parts[1].strip()
        body = parts[2].strip() if len(parts) == 3 else ""
        commits.append({"sha": sha, "subject": subject, "body": body})
    return commits


def parse_commit(commit: dict) -> dict | None:
    m = COMMIT_PATTERN.match(commit["subject"])
    if not m:
        return None
    result = m.groupdict()
    result["sha"] = commit["sha"][:8]
    result["body"] = commit["body"]
    result["is_breaking"] = bool(result.pop("breaking")) or \
        "BREAKING CHANGE" in commit["body"]
    return result


def prompt_hash(path: Path) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(path.read_bytes()).hexdigest()[:12]


def prompt_diffs(from_ref: str, prompts_dir: str = "prompts") -> list[dict]:
    """Return list of {name, before_hash, after_hash} for changed prompt files."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "diff", "--name-only", from_ref, "HEAD", "--", prompts_dir],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    diffs = []
    for path_str in result.stdout.splitlines():
        path = Path(path_str)
        if not path.exists():
            continue
        after = prompt_hash(path)
        before_bytes = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "show", f"{from_ref}:{path_str}"],
            capture_output=True
        ).stdout
        before = hashlib.sha256(before_bytes).hexdigest()[:12] if before_bytes else "(new)"
        diffs.append({"name": path.name, "before": before, "after": after})
    return diffs


def eval_delta(from_ref: str, results_path: str = "eval-results.json") -> list[dict]:
    """Compare eval-results.json between from_ref and HEAD."""
    try:
        prev_bytes = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "show", f"{from_ref}:{results_path}"],
            capture_output=True
        ).stdout
        prev = json.loads(prev_bytes) if prev_bytes else {}
        curr = json.loads(Path(results_path).read_bytes()) if Path(results_path).exists() else {}
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, FileNotFoundError):
        return []
    deltas = []
    for suite, curr_val in curr.items():
        prev_val = prev.get(suite, None)
        if prev_val is None:
            deltas.append({"suite": suite, "before": None, "after": curr_val})
        else:
            delta = round(curr_val - prev_val, 3)
            if delta != 0:
                deltas.append({"suite": suite, "before": prev_val, "after": curr_val, "delta": delta})
    return deltas


def build_changelog(version: str, from_ref: str, to_ref: str) -> str:
    commits = git_log_since(from_ref, to_ref)
    sections: dict[str, list[str]] = {s: [] for s in SECTION_ORDER}

    for raw in commits:
        parsed = parse_commit(raw)
        if parsed is None:
            continue
        section = "Breaking Changes" if parsed["is_breaking"] \
            else TYPE_TO_SECTION.get(parsed["type"])
        if section is None:
            continue
        scope_prefix = f"**{parsed['scope']}**: " if parsed["scope"] else ""
        entry = f"- {scope_prefix}{parsed['subject']} ({parsed['sha']})"
        sections[section].append(entry)

    # Append prompt diffs to Prompt Changes
    for diff in prompt_diffs(from_ref):
        sections["Prompt Changes"].append(
            f"- `{diff['name']}`: `{diff['before']}` → `{diff['after']}`"
        )

    # Append eval deltas to Eval Metrics
    for d in eval_delta(from_ref):
        if d.get("before") is None:
            line = f"- {d['suite']}: new suite, pass rate {d['after']:.1%}"
        else:
            sign = "+" if d["delta"] > 0 else ""
            regression = " ⚠️ regression" if d["delta"] < 0 else ""
            line = (f"- {d['suite']}: {d['before']:.1%} → {d['after']:.1%} "
                    f"({sign}{d['delta']*100:.1f}pp){regression}")
        sections["Eval Metrics"].append(line)

    today = date.today().isoformat()
    lines = [f"## [{version}] — {today}\n"]
    for section in SECTION_ORDER:
        if sections[section]:
            lines.append(f"### {section}\n")
            lines.extend(sections[section])
            lines.append("")

    return "\n".join(lines)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--from", dest="from_ref", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--to", dest="to_ref", default="HEAD")
    parser.add_argument("--version", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    content = build_changelog(args.version, args.from_ref, args.to_ref)
    changelog = Path("CHANGELOG.md")
    existing = changelog.read_text() if changelog.exists() else ""
    changelog.write_text(content + "\n" + existing)
    print(content)
GitHub Actions Workflow for Automated Changelog
The workflow below runs on every push to main and on tag pushes. It generates the changelog, commits it back to main, creates a GitHub Release for tag events, and enforces the quality gate. The
permissions are the minimum required; the release step needs

# .github/workflows/changelog.yml
name: Changelog & Release

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*.*.*']

permissions:
  contents: write
  releases: write
  id-token: write

jobs:
  changelog:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0          # full history required for git log
          token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Determine previous tag
        id: prev
        run: |
          PREV=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 HEAD^ 2>/dev/null || echo "")
          echo "tag=${PREV}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Determine version
        id: ver
        run: |
          if [[ "$GITHUB_REF" == refs/tags/* ]]; then
            echo "version=${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          else
            echo "version=$(date +%Y.%-m.%-d)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          fi

      - name: Run eval suite
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements-eval.txt
          python scripts/run-evals.py --output eval-results.json

      - name: Generate changelog
        id: gen
        run: |
          FROM="${{ steps.prev.outputs.tag }}"
          VERSION="${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}"
          if [ -z "$FROM" ]; then FROM="$(git rev-list --max-parents=0 HEAD)"; fi
          python scripts/generate-changelog.py \
            --from "$FROM" --version "$VERSION" > release-notes.md
          echo "empty=$(wc -l < release-notes.md)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Quality gate — fail on empty changelog
        run: |
          LINES="${{ steps.gen.outputs.empty }}"
          if [ "$LINES" -lt 5 ]; then
            echo "ERROR: changelog is effectively empty (${LINES} lines). "
            echo "Ensure commits follow Conventional Commits and include at "
            echo "least one feat, fix, prompt, safety, or eval entry."
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Commit CHANGELOG.md
        run: |
          git config user.name  "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git add CHANGELOG.md
          git diff --cached --quiet || \
            git commit -m "chore(changelog): update for ${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }} [skip ci]"
          git push

      - name: Create GitHub Release (tags only)
        if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/')
        run: |
          gh release create "${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}" \
            --title "${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}" \
            --notes-file release-notes.md
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
Standard Changelog Format for GenAI Services
The section ordering inside each release entry follows a deliberate priority: safety-critical information appears first so it is never skimmed over. The recommended order is:
— refusals added, guardrails tightened, scope restrictions. A reader scanning only the first section will always see if behaviour was made safer.
— anything that changes the public interface or breaks existing integrations. Includes prompt output format changes, removed tool signatures, and model changes that affect structured outputs.
— new capabilities, tools, or output modes.
— prompt file names, before/after hashes, and one-line descriptions.
— corrected logic errors, malformed outputs, excessive token usage.
— pass rate changes per named suite, with deltas and regression flags.
— model ID before and after, with a note on expected behavioural drift.
— integration guide updates, API reference corrections.
Sections with no entries are omitted entirely rather than printed with empty bodies. A changelog reader should never see "### Eval Metrics" followed by nothing — that signals a tooling failure.
Versioning Strategy: SemVer vs CalVer for AI Services

Semantic Versioning (SemVer: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) and Calendar Versioning (CalVer: YYYY.MM.DD or YYYY.0M.MICRO) each have a natural home in AI service development. The choice affects how changelog sections map to version numbers.

SemVer suits AI services with a stable, versioned public API — a document processing service, a code generation endpoint, a structured extraction API. The MAJOR bump for breaking changes (prompt format changes that alter structured outputs, tool signature changes) gives API consumers a clear signal to update their integration. The MINOR bump for new features and model updates signals new capability without breaking changes. SemVer also integrates naturally with semantic-release and release-please.

CalVer suits AI services that ship continuously without a fixed API contract — internal research tools, chatbots with fluid prompts, experimental evaluation pipelines. When there is no external interface to break, MAJOR/MINOR distinctions carry little meaning; the release date communicates freshness. CalVer changelogs still use the same section ordering; they simply label each release by date rather than by impact severity.
when external integrations depend on predictable output format or tool signatures.
when the service has no formal API contract and ships daily or weekly.
is ambiguous (CalVer YYYY.M.D or SemVer MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH?). Choose one and document the convention in CONTRIBUTING.md.
Breaking Change Detection in AI Changelogs

A breaking change in an AI service is not always a code-level interface break. An API consumer that parses JSON output from a summarisation endpoint can be broken just as badly by a prompt change that adds a new key, removes an existing key, or renames a field. The challenge is that these structural changes are not visible in the TypeScript or Python type signatures — they live in the prompt and in the model's learned behaviour.
Three mechanisms detect breaking changes in AI service commits. The first is the conventional commit
footer — this is the developer explicitly declaring the break. The second is the exclamation mark shorthand:

triggers a major version bump without requiring a footer. The third is an optional schema diff check in the changelog pipeline: if the tool call signatures defined in the prompt registry differ from those in the previous tag, the generator flags a breaking change automatically even if the commit message does not declare one.
(JSON field names, field types, added required fields) must use
(renamed parameters, changed required/optional status) must use the
that are known to change structured output behaviour should also be flagged as breaking in the changelog even if not technically a MAJOR SemVer bump.
release-please Configuration for GenAI Commit Types

release-please is a Google-maintained tool that reads conventional commits from a GitHub repository and manages release PRs automatically. By default it recognises the standard commit types. Adding the AI-specific types requires a configuration file.
flag keeps chore and refactor commits out of the published changelog while still processing them for version bumping. The
list tells release-please which additional files to update when it bumps the version number in
Changelog for Internal vs External Audiences
A single generated changelog often needs to serve two distinct audiences with different information needs. The

is for engineers and teams who deploy and integrate with the service. It includes prompt hashes, eval dashboard links, model IDs, token usage changes, and internal scope names. Readers understand the internal architecture.

is for external consumers — developers who call the service's HTTP API or read structured outputs. It describes capability changes in plain language without exposing internal prompt names or evaluation infrastructure. It never includes hash values or scope names that are meaningless outside the service's codebase.
The simplest implementation is two rendering passes over the same structured changelog data: one that includes all fields, and one that filters to feature changes and user-visible bug fixes. The
outputs only Safety, Breaking Changes, and Features, with prompt hash lines omitted.
commit subject should explain the intent of the prompt change in terms a non-engineer can understand, not a description of the diff. A commit subject like
prompt(summariser): change line 12 to add disclaimer
is useless to a product manager or API consumer. A subject like
prompt(summariser): add mandatory medical disclaimer to all health-topic summaries

For significant prompt changes, the commit body should explain the motivation. The changelog generator extracts the commit body and includes it as an indented paragraph under the hash line in the Prompt Changes section. This turns the changelog entry into a mini design note rather than just a hash pair.
A well-formed prompt commit looks like this in full:

prompt(summariser): add mandatory medical disclaimer to all health-topic summaries

Regulatory guidance requires that any summary touching on health, medication,
or diagnoses includes a disclaimer advising professional consultation.
Previous prompt hash: a3f9c12b4e17
New prompt hash:      7d2e8a1f903c

Affects: /prompts/summariser-system.txt
Eval impact: medical-safety suite pass rate expected to increase from 89% to 96%
The inline hash values in the commit body are redundant once the generator adds them programmatically — but including them here makes the commit self-documenting when read via
Linking Changelog Entries to Eval Dashboards

A changelog entry that says "summarisation-accuracy: 82.1% → 86.3% (+4.2pp)" is useful. One that also links to the full eval run — showing the distribution of failures, the specific test cases that changed, and the confidence interval — is far more actionable.
GitHub Actions artifacts provide a stable URL pattern for eval results:
. The changelog generator can embed this URL alongside each eval entry by reading the
environment variable and the artifact ID returned by the
step. Alternatively, if eval results are published to an external dashboard (MLflow, Weights & Biases, a custom tool), the eval run URL can be embedded in
field and the generator picks it up automatically.
Storing Changelogs: CHANGELOG.md vs GitHub Releases vs Notion
Three common storage targets exist for changelog content, each with trade-offs:
— version-controlled, visible in the repo, easy to diff and audit. Developers can read changelog history locally with
. Requires a bot commit per release, which pollutes the commit graph slightly. Best for open-source projects or any project where the changelog must be auditable as code.

— attached to a tag, visible in the GitHub UI, accessible via the GitHub API. Easy to publish release assets alongside the notes. Does not appear in the repository file tree. Best for projects where operators primarily consume releases through the GitHub UI or the

— suitable for operator changelog that includes embargoed information or links to internal dashboards. The release pipeline can POST to the Notion API to create a new page per release. Search and cross-linking work well. Downside: not version-controlled, and coupling the CI pipeline to an external SaaS introduces a new failure mode.

For most AI service teams, the right combination is CHANGELOG.md in the repository (for audit and code review) plus a GitHub Release (for visible, API-accessible release notes). The Notion or wiki target is supplementary for internal stakeholders who are not comfortable with GitHub.
Automation Quality Gate: Reject Releases Without Meaningful Changelog

The pipeline should refuse to create a release if the generated changelog contains no meaningful entries. This quality gate addresses the scenario where a developer pushes directly to main without following conventional commit conventions — the resulting release would have an empty changelog, providing no information to downstream consumers.
The gate checks three conditions before allowing the release to proceed:
The changelog contains at least one entry in a visible section
(Safety, Features, Prompt Changes, Bug Fixes, Eval Metrics, Breaking Changes, or Model Version). Chore and doc-only changes do not count as a meaningful release.
If an eval section is present, no eval suite shows a regression beyond the configured threshold
(e.g., no single suite drops more than 2 percentage points). A release with a significant eval regression should be held until the cause is understood.
If BREAKING CHANGE entries are present, the version bump is MAJOR
(for SemVer). A breaking change merged without a MAJOR bump is a silent contract violation.

The GitHub Actions step for the gate is a simple shell check, as shown in the workflow above. A more sophisticated implementation uses a dedicated Python script that reads the parsed changelog data structure and asserts each condition, printing a human-readable explanation of which check failed and what the developer needs to fix.
Prompt changes committed without the prompt type
commit is invisible to the changelog generator. Pre-commit hooks that check for
changes in the prompts directory and require the
in the bot commit — pre-commit hooks that run on the bot commit will loop if they check the same files the bot is committing. Use
in the commit message or configure commitlint to ignore the bot author.
Eval regression threshold set too tight

— a 0.1pp regression block causes the pipeline to fail on normal stochastic variance in eval results. Set thresholds at 1–2pp for most suites, and reserve the 0.5pp threshold for safety-critical evals only.
requires the full commit history between the two refs.
Not pinning the from-ref when there is no previous tag
returns an error. The workflow must handle this by falling back to the initial commit hash.
Enforce commit conventions at the PR level
using a PR title lint action (
) so that squash-merged PRs produce well-formed commit messages automatically.
Run the changelog generator in preview mode on every PR
— not to commit, but to post a comment showing what the release notes would look like if the PR were merged. Developers can see immediately whether their commit type is correct.
Store eval-results.json in the repository at a stable path
(not only as a CI artifact) so the changelog generator can access it reliably when diffing against a previous tag via
Use signed commits for the changelog bot
— the bot commit updating CHANGELOG.md should be signed with the GitHub Actions token to distinguish automated changelog commits from human commits in the audit log.
Keep the changelog generator under version control and lint it
— it is part of the release infrastructure. A bug in the generator that omits prompt changes silently is as damaging as a bug in the service itself.
AI service changelogs must capture prompt content hashes, evaluation metric deltas, and model version changes — not just code-level changes — because behaviour is governed by all three.
Conventional Commits provide the machine-readable type and scope that the changelog generator uses to route entries into sections. AI-specific types (

The Prompt Changes section should list the prompt file name with before and after SHA-256 content hashes so operators can verify which prompt version is running and reproduce behaviour by pinning to a hash.
The Eval Metrics section surfaces pass rate changes as concrete signals: improvements show that the change is working, regressions trigger the quality gate and block the release.
Section ordering (Safety first, then Breaking Changes, Features, Prompt Changes, Bug Fixes, Eval Metrics, Model Version, Docs) ensures the most critical information is never buried.
SemVer is appropriate when external integrations depend on stable output formats; CalVer suits continuously deployed services without formal API contracts.

The quality gate blocks a release when the changelog is empty (indicating all commits lacked conventional types), when a safety eval regresses beyond threshold, or when a breaking change is present without a corresponding MAJOR version bump.

Separate operator and public renderings from the same structured changelog data — operators need prompt hashes and eval dashboard links; external API consumers need only capability changes in plain language.
